critique.summary: The article provides a detailed overview of the new features, previews, and deprecations introduced in Java 26, including enhancements to the HotSpot JVM, HTTP Client API supporting HTTP/3, structured concurrency improvements, lazy constants, and updates to pattern matching. It also discusses the preparation for future features related to Project Valhalla and the tightening of final field mutation rules.
mainPoints: Java 26 introduces new HotSpot JVM features such as ahead-of-time object caching compatible with any garbage collector (JEP 516) and improved G1 garbage collector throughput by reducing synchronization (JEP 522).
The HTTP Client API now supports HTTP/3 (JEP 517) with multiple negotiation strategies for protocol fallback.

Several preview features are reintroduced with minor changes, including PEM encoding for cryptographic objects (JEP 524), structured concurrency enhancements (JEP 525), lazy constants for deferred immutable initialization (JEP 526), and primitive types support in pattern matching (JEP 530).
The Vector API continues incubation (JEP 529) for efficient SIMD computations, awaiting integration with Project Valhalla features.
Java 26 deprecates and prepares to restrict mutation of final fields via deep reflection to improve program correctness and JVM optimizations (JEP 500).
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately summarizes the official Java Enhancement Proposals (JEPs) and their statuses as of Java 26, consistent with publicly available OpenJDK documentation and release notes. It provides correct technical explanations and examples for the features introduced or previewed in Java 26. The claims about feature statuses, preview requirements, and future plans align with the OpenJDK project roadmap and JEP documents. The article does not present unsupported or false claims and is factually reliable regarding Java 26 content.
considerations: The article assumes familiarity with Java internals and JEP terminology, which may limit accessibility for less experienced readers.
It does not discuss potential backward compatibility issues or migration challenges developers might face when adopting these new features.
The performance benefits described, especially for JVM and concurrency improvements, may vary depending on application workloads and environments.
The article does not provide perspectives from independent experts or user feedback on the new features' practical impact.
Security implications of changes, such as deprecating the Applet API or final field mutation restrictions, are not deeply explored.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around Java 26's new features and the language's evolution over time. Commenters reflect on Java's historical challenges, cultural issues, and improvements, while also touching on Android's lag in adopting new Java versions and the language's ecosystem.

comments.author: ludovicianul
comments.text: I program in Java for more than 15 years now. I can resonate with people hating the language from it's early days due to the experience with all the enterprisy features and over abstractions. Or confunding Java with the Spring ecosystem. But Java came a long way over the years. It's now what many would call a "modern" language. It's less verbose, has many of the features people find appealing in Scala and Kotlin and it can even compile to native binaries using GraalVM. This made building CLIs in Java feasible. Or lambdas.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced and experienced perspective on Java's evolution, highlighting concrete improvements and modern features, making it highly accurate and informative.

comments.author: einrealist
comments.text: I have been using Java since version 1.4. Both the language and its ecosystem have come a long way since then. I endured the height of the EJB phase. I adopted Spring when version 1.2 was released. I spent hours fighting with IDEs to run OSGi bundles. I hated building UIs with Swing/AWT, many of which are still in use today and are gradually being replaced by lovely JavaFX. When I look at code I wrote around 12 years ago, I'm amazed at how much I've matured too.
comments.analysis: Shares a detailed personal history with Java and its ecosystem, providing context on past challenges and growth, which adds credibility and depth to the discussion.

comments.author: xeubie
comments.text:
I think astronomers could measure the age of the universe in nano-Valhallas. Every year, it feels 50% closer to completion...

In all seriousness I'm happy with what Mr. Goetz and the team have done. Sealed interfaces (java 17) + exhaustive switch statements (java 21) means we now have union types in java! And instead of jumping on the async/await bandwagon we now have a more general solution that doesn't lead to API duplication (virtual threads). But Valhalla has been a veeery long time coming.
comments.analysis: Accurately summarizes recent Java language features and their significance, showing understanding of technical details and improvements.

comments.author: vyskocilm
comments.text:
JEP 504: Remove the Applet API

Glad to see this being removed. Java plugins especially on Linux were awful and required by tons of corporate stuff. Anyone remeber IcedTea Web? A functional and opensource Java plugin and Java Webstart implementation?
comments.analysis: Correctly notes the removal of the Applet API and reflects on its problematic history, providing useful context for the change.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
the people that work on Java & the JVM are very smart.

it has become a best of breed language - hell its better than Go for industry purposes.

the drawback with Java will always be the CULTURE - (maybe someone can insert a quote of how in physics progress is only made, when old physicist die - I don't wanna be morbid ) but with Java same that's when the culture will change.

All those people using typescript (could be using Java - but the culture doesn't want them and consider them heretics for not embracing religion of OOP and FactoryFactory)
comments.analysis: Offers an opinion on Java's cultural challenges and community resistance, which is subjective but grounded in observable industry attitudes.

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: As someone who has been out of the Java world for many years, but recently forced back into it due to Android dev requirements, Post lawsuit, what is the relationship between Android (Google) and Java now?  When can we expect 26 on Android?  On that note, why is Android always so far behind?  Is it because Kotlin is primary or is it deeper?  Did the lawsuit play a role?
comments.analysis: Raises valid questions about Android's lag in Java adoption and the impact of legal issues, reflecting common concerns in the ecosystem.

comments.author: larrylawnmower
comments.text: Are people still using gigantic xml file abominations (like pom.xml) with java? No thanks. Web moved years ago, and now most LOB software appear to also have switched to languages that are not that heavy weight and riddled with software patterns. And then the sdk still require accepting an EULA. Gtfo.
comments.analysis: Critiques Java's build system and ecosystem complexity, which is a common criticism, though somewhat generalized.

comments.author: Bnjoroge
comments.text: Man I still have scars learning Java in college. Took the entire fun out of coding until I ended up learning C.
comments.analysis: Expresses personal frustration with learning Java, which is subjective and less informative about the language's current state.

comments.author: OSaMaBiNLoGiN
comments.text:
All the changes look great. But I don't know how I feel about the syntax. A lot of things that very well could be first-class just aren't. Instead of a `lazy` keyword, we get `LazyConstant<T>`.

I'm sure there's reasons as to why. I just don't know them.
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced view on Java's syntax design choices, indicating awareness of language design trade-offs.

comments.author: nameconflicts
comments.text: I think Java became popular because of Sun. My experience with Java-based apps is bad. Laggy, resource-heavy, IDE bound, and it causes premature hardware performance degradation.
comments.analysis: Offers a negative personal opinion on Java applications' performance and tooling, which may be anecdotal and lacks technical detail.
